text: Every household should maintain a kit that sustains each family member for at least 72 hours. Store the kit in an accessible location and check it every six months. Essential contents per person: 1 gallon of water per day (3 gallons total), non-perishable food (canned goods, energy bars, dried fruit, peanut butter), manual can opener, battery-powered or hand-crank radio, flashlight with extra batteries, first aid kit, whistle for signaling, dust masks, plastic sheeting and duct tape for shelter-in-place, moist towelettes, garbage bags and ties, wrench or pliers for turning off utilities, local maps, and cell phone charger (battery bank or solar).

Store copies of critical documents in a waterproof bag within your emergency kit: identification (driver's license, passport), insurance policies, bank account records, medication list and prescriptions, family contact information, proof of address, and birth certificates. Take photos of these documents on your phone as a backup. Consider storing digital copies in an encrypted cloud service or on a USB drive in a separate location.

For home water storage beyond 72 hours, store 1 gallon per person per day for a minimum of 2 weeks (14 gallons per person). Use food-grade water storage containers — never containers that previously held chemicals. Commercial water jugs, cleaned 2-liter soda bottles, and dedicated water storage barrels (15-55 gallons) all work. Add 1/8 teaspoon of unscented household bleach per gallon if storing tap water for long periods. Replace stored water every 6-12 months. A water heater tank (typically 40-80 gallons) is an emergency water source — turn off the intake valve and power before draining.
section: Emergency Preparedness Kits > Water Storage for Extended Emergencies

text: Keep flashlights and batteries in every room. Battery-powered lanterns are safer than candles indoors. A hand-crank or solar-powered radio provides emergency broadcasts when power is out. If using a generator, run it outdoors only — never in a garage, basement, or enclosed area due to carbon monoxide risk. Place carbon monoxide detectors on every level of your home. In winter, close off unused rooms to conserve heat. Open faucets to a slow drip to prevent pipe freezing. Your refrigerator keeps food safe for 4 hours without power if the door stays closed; a full freezer maintains temperature for 48 hours (24 if half full).
section: Emergency Preparedness Kits > Power Outage Preparedness

text: Establish two evacuation routes from your home and neighborhood. Identify a local meeting point (a neighbor's house or nearby landmark) and an out-of-area contact person everyone can call. Keep your vehicle fuel tank at least half full at all times. Pack a go-bag with essentials: the items from your 72-hour kit plus sturdy shoes, weather-appropriate clothing, cash in small bills, medications, and copies of important documents. Know your community's evacuation routes and shelter locations. Practice the plan with your family at least twice a year.

In some emergencies (chemical spills, radiation events, severe storms), sheltering in place is safer than evacuating. Choose an interior room with no windows, or as few as possible, on the highest floor (for chemical threats — chemicals are often heavier than air). Seal doors, windows, and vents with plastic sheeting and duct tape. Turn off HVAC systems to prevent drawing contaminated air inside. Listen to emergency broadcasts for instructions on when it is safe to ventilate. Keep at least 2 days of water and food in your shelter-in-place location.
section: Emergency Preparedness Kits > Sheltering in Place

text: A prolonged power outage (days to weeks) fundamentally changes daily life. Priorities shift to water, food preservation, heating/cooling, communication, and security. Immediately fill bathtubs and large containers with water while municipal pressure holds. Use perishable food first — eat refrigerated items within 4 hours, then move to frozen food, then shelf-stable supplies. Cook perishable food before it spoils. In winter, move the family into one room and use body heat, blankets, and safe heat sources (wood stove, kerosene heater with ventilation).

When cell towers and internet fail, alternative communication options include: battery-powered AM/FM radio for emergency broadcasts, NOAA weather radio (7 channels with continuous updates), FRS/GMRS two-way radios (range 1-5 miles typical), ham radio (requires license but provides long-range communication), and physical message boards at community gathering points. Keep a hand-crank or solar radio — batteries will eventually run out. Program emergency frequencies into radios in advance. Establish a communication plan with neighbors: specific check-in times, signal flags, or designated meeting spots.

When the grid goes down, preserve food using non-electric methods. Smoking meats over a low fire (130-170°F) both cooks and preserves — cold-smoked meats last weeks in cool weather. Salt-curing uses 8 pounds of salt per 100 pounds of meat and preserves for months. Root cellaring stores vegetables at 32-40°F using the earth's natural temperature — dig below the frost line. Dehydrating food in the sun or over a fire removes moisture — slice thin (1/4 inch) for even drying. Lacto-fermentation preserves vegetables for months using only salt and water. Rendering fat (lard, tallow) preserves it for months at room temperature.
section: Grid-Down Scenarios > Food Preservation Without Refrigeration

text: Human waste management becomes critical when plumbing fails. A 5-gallon bucket with a seat and heavy-duty garbage bags serves as an emergency toilet. After each use, add a scoop of sawdust, wood ash, or cat litter to reduce odor and absorb moisture. Seal full bags tightly and store away from living areas and water sources. Dig a latrine trench at least 200 feet from water sources, 6-8 inches deep — deeper in cold weather to prevent freezing the surface. Wash hands with water and soap after every use — hand hygiene prevents the waterborne diseases that become epidemic during infrastructure failures.
section: Grid-Down Scenarios > Sanitation Without Running Water

text: Isolated individuals are vulnerable. During extended emergencies, organize with neighbors: pool resources, share skills, coordinate security, and distribute tasks. Identify who has medical training, mechanical skills, gardening knowledge, and ham radio equipment. Establish a watch schedule. Share surplus food and water with those in need — community cohesion is a survival resource. Historical disasters consistently show that organized communities recover faster and with fewer casualties than disorganized ones.

During shaking: Drop, Cover, Hold On. Drop to hands and knees, get under a sturdy desk or table and hold on, or cover your head and neck with your arms if no shelter is available. Stay away from windows, exterior walls, and heavy objects that could fall. If in bed, stay there and protect your head with a pillow. Do not run outside during shaking — most injuries occur from falling debris near exits. If outdoors, move to an open area away from buildings, trees, and power lines. After shaking stops: check for injuries, check for gas leaks (smell, but do not use flames — use a flashlight), and be prepared for aftershocks. Do not re-enter damaged buildings.

Never drive through floodwater — 6 inches of moving water can knock you down, 12 inches can carry away a small car, and 2 feet will float most vehicles. If flooding is predicted: move valuables to upper floors, fill bathtubs and containers with clean water, and be ready to evacuate. If ordered to evacuate, do so immediately — take your go-bag and follow designated evacuation routes. If trapped by rising water: go to the highest point available, signal for help, and call emergency services. After floodwater recedes: do not enter flooded buildings until authorities confirm safety. Floodwater is contaminated — assume anything it touched is contaminated. Discard food that contacted floodwater. Disinfect all surfaces with a bleach solution (1 cup per 5 gallons of water).

Create defensible space around your home: Zone 1 (0-5 feet from structure) — use noncombustible materials, remove dead vegetation, keep gutters clean. Zone 2 (5-30 feet) — space trees 10+ feet apart, remove ladder fuels (vegetation that allows fire to climb from ground to treetops), keep grass mowed to 4 inches. Zone 3 (30-100 feet) — thin trees to 10-foot spacing, remove dead wood and debris. If a wildfire approaches: close all windows, doors, and vents. Move flammable furniture away from windows. Leave lights on (visibility for firefighters in smoke). Connect garden hoses. Fill pools, hot tubs, and garbage cans with water. If ordered to evacuate, leave immediately — wildfires can outrun a person and move faster than you expect.

Before winter: insulate pipes, service your heating system, stock 3+ days of food and water, ensure you have alternative heat (wood stove, kerosene heater — with ventilation). During a winter storm: stay indoors. If you must go outside, wear multiple layers (wicking base, insulating middle, waterproof outer), cover extremities, and limit exposure time. Frostbite warning signs: numbness, white or grayish-yellow skin, hard or waxy feeling. Treatment: get indoors, warm the affected area gradually in warm (not hot) water (100-105°F), do not rub frostbitten skin. If pipes freeze: open the affected faucet, apply gentle heat (hair dryer, heat lamp, warm towels) starting from the faucet end and working toward the frozen section. Never use an open flame on pipes.
section: Natural Disaster Response > Winter Storm Survival

text: Know the difference: a tornado watch means conditions are favorable for tornado formation (be prepared); a tornado warning means a tornado has been sighted or detected on radar (take shelter immediately). Shelter priorities: underground (basement, storm cellar) is best. If no underground option, go to the lowest floor interior room (closet, bathroom, hallway) away from windows. Get under a sturdy piece of furniture and cover your head. In a mobile home, leave and go to a sturdy building — mobile homes are extremely dangerous in tornadoes regardless of tie-downs. If caught outside with no shelter, lie flat in a low ditch and cover your head. Do not shelter under highway overpasses — wind accelerates through these structures.
